I want you to understand the philosophy of looping because it's going to make it so much easier for you to use once you really understand what's going on. So remember now we look backwards, right? So here we are. We're at the end of the front half of the sale. You asked for the order for the first time and they said, let me think about it. Let me call you back. Bad time of year. I'm not liquid right now. Got to speak to my partner and say it's close to the tooth. Right. And the reason I'm only going to focus on those people is because for those that say yes, right, you just close the deal. Good for you. You now have a new client and there's a whole set of rules now for how we turn that first time client into a long term client. Essentially, you're raving fan advice. You would get it again. It gives you referrals, right? That's one group. We're not going to talk about today. Second group of those that say no, not interested. And again, you know what to do with those. We're not in the business of turning nos into yeses, right? So if that's what they say, then they slip through, they were tie kickers. You want to not waste your time trying to turn a hard no into a yes. That's not what looping was designed for, which is why I'm only focusing now on this third group of the ones that hit you with a common objection, right? So let's focus on them. How do you do that? Very simple. I want you to imagine where you are in the sale right now. At the end of the front half, we get hit the first objection, right? And now I want you to kind of imagine the straight line, walk down the straight line to the end when they say yes. And now say, okay, looking backwards to that point where I was, where they said, let me think about it. What has to happen to get them to move from let me, well, let's just use let me think about it. What has to happen to get them from let me think about it to yes. That's what the back half of the sale is for. Right. So we look back now, they just said yes. Well, what happened? Why would someone change their mind? Well, the obvious answer is, is that you told them things about your product that made it more attractive in their mind and explain why it was the best deal that perhaps made them very certain that maybe if something went wrong with it, you'd be there to help them or there'd be a money back guarantee or whatever it might be. You gave them more information that impulse them to change their mind. They said, let me think about it, right? Which is a smoke stream for uncertainty. So essentially you made them more certain through your words, your tonality, your body language and that increased certainty caused them to change their mind. That's what happened backwards. Looks like at the end now. They just said, yes, great. Well, before they would say, let me think about what happened. You said things to them. You handed them documents. You pointed them to websites. Maybe they checked around if it's a longer sale cycle. The point was, is that there are certain things that went on when someone goes from let me think about it to yes, it's not an accident. It's not like someone says, let me think about it. And you say, you know, I've been doing this for a long time. And you know, when people think about it, they typically put it in the box. They put it in the back of their mind. They decide against it. I know you like it. I don't want you to do that right now. You just give me one. That doesn't change anyone's mind to tell someone why they don't need to really think about it. Or why people think about it and the coming and last in life. Or why, you know, call me back because that's a strategy that's really sustainable for them because they're too busy. They don't call you. You're busy. They're busy. That stuff, those were buttocks to the objections. That's not where the magic lies in the straight line. It's not the magic lies in selling. Remember, look backwards. What had to happen for someone to change their mind? The answers you gave them new information. You said things to them. You showed them documents. You made some type of airtight logical case from the frame. Remember, you start with a frame and then you ask for the order and they say, let me think about it. And because you framed and did it in front load, you have your best stuff right now to throw at them to sort of fill in the blank. So they say, oh, now I get it. That the purpose of your first loop is that when you're done with that second language, pan that that first look does your first pattern is what your main presentation. When they say, let me think about and you do your first loop and you hit them with that second language pattern. That language pattern must end with them saying, ah, now I get it. Now it makes more sense to me on how I see why it's more valuable. I'm not buying yet, but now I like this. So while you said to them, you know, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Right? That's your how you open the loop. And they say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. When you close out this first pattern and say, you see what I'm saying now, do you like it? Like, oh, yeah. Yes. Now I see because you gave them more information. I can't overestimate the importance of this follow up pattern on some levels. This is more important than the first. You know, it's not that it's more important, but it's that the first is a framing pattern. And now this is a lamb. I'm going to really wow. Oh boy, that's my best stuff. And then again, with the one that comes after, even I just like to cherry on top. Remember certainty wanes. Even after 30 seconds, so it's, oh, I love the idea. 30 seconds. They're not sure human beings. That's how we're built certainty wanes until something becomes a conviction. We tend to go back and forth with our certainty. We'll get very, very certain and then certainties lost to fade. So when we loop, what we're doing is we go. We loop back into presentation mode, right? And we give them new information that raises their level of certainty. I want you to focus on this first pattern today. Just just this, just this first pattern right now. Forget it. None of your farce gun panel. Handle your forest gun pattern tomorrow. I want to handle this pattern by pattern. Make sure that today, OK, which is going to be, I think, for everyone. That's your job for today. Don't even. And obviously you keep running. You know the street. Once you run your forest gun pattern, right? So you hit all three tens in this first loop, right? But for today, your mini homework, that homework is for you, right? Is about making sure that the actual word pattern, the language pattern you constructed is awesome because usually just, you know, when I find someone who's using the straight line, but not, you know, they always have some success, right? Some stuff's just easy. The tonality of works, right? But they're not having that ultra amazing success. It's typically because they did not take the time to build their patterns. They didn't take that time to have that strategic preparation. So they really knew what they were going to say before they said it and they were awesome.
